summary: Gruber argues that Apple's decision not to make a low-cost iPhone for China is strategically sound, not a blunder. He cites data showing that 2% of China's population consumes one-third of the world's luxury goods, and that this wealthy class is growing rapidly. Rather than competing on price against commodity Android devices, Apple is positioning itself as attainable luxury — the same strategy it uses everywhere. The hirings of executives from Burberry and Yves Saint Laurent signal Apple's intent to strengthen its luxury brand positioning, particularly relevant to the Chinese market.
key_points: 27 million Chinese consumers account for one-third of global luxury goods purchases, and this class is growing fast
The iPhone 5C's non-cheap pricing was widely misread as ignoring China, when it was actually aligned with Apple's luxury positioning there
From Apple's perspective there are no 'emerging markets' — only people who can and can't afford their products
Hiring luxury brand executives from Burberry and YSL signals Apple is doubling down on premium positioning, not moving downmarket
Burberry has 69 retail stores in China compared to Apple's eight, suggesting massive room for retail expansion
money_quotes: They're skating to where the puck is heading, not where it is, and positioning their products to thrive as China's upper class grows.
From Apple's perspective, there's no such thing as an "emerging market".
There are certainly cultural differences between consumers in different countries, but the bottom line is that there are people who can afford iPhones and iPads, and people who can't.
The class of people who can afford Apple products is growing faster in China than it is anywhere else.

Apple's path to success in China is not to lower itself and compete purely on price against low-cost commodity Android devices. It is the opposite: to further separate its products, in terms of branding and quality, from its competition.
The sweet spot for Apple in China is the same as it is in the U.S. and elsewhere: attainable, affordable, casual luxury.
key_insight: Apple's refusal to chase the low-cost market in China is not a strategic mistake but a deliberate bet on the rapidly growing Chinese luxury consumer class.
